Vars  
What Is a Process?  
• A process is a running instance of a program  
• A process has its own virtual address space managed by MMU.  
Stack  
Heap  
• Contains:  
• Code (text segment)  
• Data & BSS  
• Heap  
• Stack  
• File descriptors  
• Environment variables  
• Registers, PC, SP  
BSS  
(uninitialized)  
• Every process is identified by:  
• PID (Process ID)  
• PPID (Parent PID)  
• Group ID  
Data  
(initialized)  
Text (code)  
• Session ID  
Intension  
One  
Break bigger  
performing  
particular  
operation  
task into  
smaller once  
Make  
application  
design  
Concurrency  
Examples:  
simple  
• Logging process  
• communication process  
• sensor reader process  
Process States  
Typical Linux process states:  
R – Running / Runnable  
S – Sleeping (interruptible)  
D – Disk sleep (uninterruptible)  
T – Stopped  
Z – Zombie (terminated but parent didn’t read exit status)  
X – Dead (rare; internal kernel use)  
Process state can be viewed via /proc/<pid>/status  
Process lifecycle  
new → ready → running → waiting → zombie.  
Parent - Child Processes  
Processes have  
Parent – Child  
relationship  
A process is  
created by its  
parent  
Processes form a tree rooted at PID 1 (init/systemd)  
Process Management – Core Operations  
System programming requires controlling:  
•
•
•
•
•
Creation: fork()  
Replacement: exec*()  
Termination: exit()  
Synchronization: wait(), waitpid()  
Information: getpid(), getppid()  
Process ID  
• Unique identifier  
• Reusable after termination  
• Header Required: #include <unistd.h>  
• pid_t getpid(void)  
• pid_t getppid(void)  
Systems calls related to ID  
Syntax  
Return Value / Errors  
pid_t getpid(void)  
pid_t getppid(void)  
uid_t getuid(void)  
uid_t geteuid(void)  
Returns calling process PID  
Returns parent PID  
Real user ID  
Effective user ID  
Sample Code – Display PID and PPID  
• Display Process ID and Parent process ID  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
int main(){  
printf("The process id is %d\n", getpid());  
printf("The parent process id is %d\n", getppid());  
}
Process Creation - fork()  
• Creates a new process running the same image as the current process  
• Creates a new PID for child.  
• The fork() system call creates a new child process by duplicating the calling (parent) process.  
After fork(), both parent and child continue execution independently.  
The child gets a copy of the parent’s memory, but they do not share stack or  
heap unless explicitly done via shared memory.  
Sample Code – Process Creation using fork()  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
int main(){  
printf("The process ID is %d\n", getpid());  
pid_t returnValue = fork();  
printf("returnValue = %d\n", returnValue);  
printf("\nAfter fork\n");  
}
Sample Output  
The process ID is 1153758  
returnValue = 1153899  
After fork  
returnValue = 0  
After fork  
Value returned by fork()  
pid_t fork(void)  
Process  
Parent  
Child  
return value of fork()  
Child PID  
0
Error  
-1  
Sample Code  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
int main(){  
printf("The process ID is %d\n", getpid());  
pid_t returnValue = fork();  
printf("returnValue = %d\n", returnValue);  
Sample Output  
if (returnValue == 0) {  
printf("Child process with ID %d\n",getpid());

returnValue = 1156160  
Parent process with ID 1156041  
After fork  
returnValue = 0  
Child process with ID 1156160  
After fork  
} else {  
printf("Parent process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
}
printf("\nAfter fork\n");  
}
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
Parent  
and  
static int global_data = 111;  
Child  
Process  
with  
int main(){  
int local_data = 222;  
printf("The process ID is %d\n", getpid());  
pid_t returnValue = fork();  
printf("returnValue = %d\n", returnValue);

and  
if (returnValue == 0) {  
local

printf("Value of Global Data = %d and Local Data = %d",global_data,local_data);  
global_data *= 2;  
local_data *= 2;

Parent and child  
have separate  
copies of global &  
local variables.  
} else {  
printf("I'm a Parent process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
printf("Value of Global Data = %d and Local Data = %d",global_data,local_data);  
}
printf("\nAfter fork\n");  
}
wait  
• Each process has entry in process table  
• Used to give information on how the child process exited  
• #include<sys/wait.h>  
pid_t wait(int *status);  
• Returns PID of the terminated child  
• Returns -1 on error  
• Stores exit information in status (if not NULL)  
What Does wait() Really Do?  
• Blocks until any child process exits  
• Reaps (removes) that child from the process table  
• Returns the child's PID  
• Stores exit status and signal info in status  
• If the parent never calls wait(), zombie processes accumulate.  
#include <stdio.h>  
Sample code – wait()  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
#include <sys/wait.h>  
int main(){  
int status = 0;  
pid_t returnValue = fork();  
if (returnValue == 0) {  
printf("I'm a Child process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
sleep(5);  
printf("Exiting from Child Process.\n");  
} else {  
Sample Output  
wait(&status);  
printf("I'm a Parent process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
I'm a Child process with ID 1186735  
Exiting from Child Process.  
I'm a Parent process with ID 1186574  
}
}
Why wait() Is Not Enough  
• wait() always waits for any child.  
• If you have multiple children, you cannot choose which one to wait for.  
• Also, wait() blocks until some child exits.  
• For more control, POSIX provides:  
waitpid()  
waitpid() — the Better Version of wait()  
pid_t waitpid(pid_t pid, int *status, int options);  
• Wait for one specific child  
• Wait for any child  
• Wait non-blocking  
• Support job control signals  
• Used in almost all real systems  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
#include <sys/wait.h>  
Sample code – waitpid()  
int main(){  
int status = 0;  
pid_t returnValue = fork();  
if (returnValue == 0) {  
printf("I'm a Child process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
sleep(5);  
printf("Exiting from Child Process.\n");  
} else {  
Sample Output  
waitpid(returnValue, &status, 0);  
printf("I'm a Parent process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
I'm a Child process with ID 1186735  
Exiting from Child Process.  
I'm a Parent process with ID 1186574  
}
}
Scenario 1: Supervisor/Watchdog Restart Loop  
• Many embedded services need to restart automatically if they crash.  
• Parent process forks a child  
• Child runs the actual service  
• Parent waits using waitpid()  
• If the child crashes → parent restarts it  
Orphan Process  
• An orphan process is a process whose parent process has terminated, while the child process continues  
to run.  
• The operating system reassigns the orphaned child to init (PID 1) or systemd in Linux systems.  
• init/systemd becomes the new parent and is responsible for:  
o Monitoring the orphan process  
o Cleaning up its resources when it finishes  
• Orphan processes themselves are not harmful. They are normal and well-handled by the OS.  
Sample Code –  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
#include <sys/wait.h>  
Orphan Process  
int main(){  
int status = 0;  
pid_t id = fork();  
if (id == 0) {  
printf("I'm a Child process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
sleep(10);  
printf("Exiting from Child Process.\n");  
} else {  
printf("I'm a Parent process with ID %d\n",getpid());  
return 0;  
}
}
Zombie Process  
• A zombie process (also called a defunct process) is:  
• A child process that has already terminated  
• But its parent has not read its exit status  
• The kernel keeps a small entry for it in the process table  
• This leftover entry allows the parent to call wait() and find out:  
• The child’s exit code  
• Whether it was terminated normally or by a signal  
• Other info communicated through the exit status  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
#include <sys/wait.h>  
Sample Code –  
Zombie Process  
int main(){  
int status = 0;  
pid_t id = fork();  
if (id == 0) {  
printf("I'm a Child process with PID %d\n",getpid());  
sleep(5);  
printf("Exiting from Child Process.\n");  
} else {  
printf("I'm a Parent process with PID %d\n",getpid());  
sleep(15);  
waitpid(id, NULL, 0);  
printf("Exiting from Child Process.\n");  
}
}
Why Zombies Can Be a Problem ?  
• One zombie is harmless — it uses almost no memory.  
• But a large number of zombies can cause:  
• PID exhaustion (you can't create new processes)  
• System instability  
• Resource leaks  
• This usually happens when:  
• Parent process never calls wait()  
Executing a New Program — exec()  
• Replaces the current process image with a new program, not PID  
• exec family: execl, execlp, execv, execve, execvp  
• Does not create a new process — PID stays the same  
• After success, the old code/data/heap/stack no longer exist  
• Used after fork() by the child process to load another program  
int execv(const char *path, char *const argv[])  
• path: path to executable;  
• argv: NULL-terminated array  
• Does not return on success. On error: -1  
Sample Code 1 – execv()  
main.c  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <errno.h>  
newfile.c  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
int main(){  
printf("This is main.c (PID=%d)\n", getpid());  
char* arg[] = {"newfile", NULL};  
execv("./newfile",arg);  
perror("execv failed");  
int main() {  
printf("Hello from newfile.c! (PID=%d,  
printf("This is still main.c because execv() failed.\n");  
PPID=%d)\n", getpid(), getppid());  
return 0;  
return 0;  
}
}
Sample Code 2 – execv()  
Passing arguments to new program  
main.c  
newfile.c  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <errno.h>  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {  
printf("Hello from newfile.c! (PID=%d,  
PPID=%d)\n", getpid(), getppid());  
int main(){  
printf("This is main.c (PID=%d)\n", getpid());  
char* arg[] = {"newfile", "alpha", "Hello There!", NULL};  
execv("./newfile",arg);  
printf("argc = %d\n", argc);  
for (int i = 0; i < argc; i++) {  
printf("argv[%d] = '%s'\n", i, argv[i]);  
}
perror("execv failed");  
printf("This is still main.c because execv() failed.\n");  
return 0;  
}
return 0;  
}
Scenario - ECU Config Snapshot Backup  
Context:  
During vehicle diagnostics, a tool exports the current ECU configuration snapshot to a text file (e.g.,  
parameter settings, feature flags, VIN, timestamps). Before running any experiments or flashing firmware,  
engineers must create a backup copy of this snapshot so they can restore the exact state if needed.  
What tiny utility does:  
• The parent process reads the entire original snapshot ecu_config_snapshot.txt.  
• It then forks one child.  
• The child writes that exact content into backup_ecu_config.txt.  
• The parent prints a message (“Child is creating a copy…”) and waits for the child.  
Summary  
• fork creates  
• exec replaces  
• wait reaps  
• zombies = un-reaped children  
• orphans adopted by PID 1  
Process Lifecycle  
Stopped  
fork  
suspend  
schedule  
resume  
run  
exit  
New  
Ready  
Running  
wait  
Zombie  
Preempt/  
reschedule  
Event/ signal  
Sleeping  
Summary: fork() Memory Diagram  
Before fork()  
Parent Process (PID = 1200)  
Text | Data | BSS | Heap | Stack | FD Table  
fork();  
After fork()  
Parent Process (PID = 1200)  
Child Process (PID = 1201)  
Text | Data | BSS  
Heap  
Text | Data | BSS  
Heap  
Shared (Copy-on-Write)separate  
copies after first write  
Stack  
Stack  
separate  
points to same Open File Table entries  
FD Table  
FD Table  
Summary: exec()  
Before exec()  
Process (PID = 2345)  
Running program: main.c  
Memory: text/data/bss/heap/stack for main.c  
FDs remain open  
exec("./newfile")  
After exec()  
Same Process (PID = 2345)  
Now Running program: newfile  
Memory replaced entirely with new program  
Only FDs survive (if not marked O_CLOEXEC)  
Summary: Zombie Process  
Parent  
|
fork()  
Child Created  
|
|
Parent running  
Child running  
|
|
NO wait()  
|
|
|
|
Child exits  
Child Entry still in  
Process Table,  
Child Becomes Zombie  
|
Parent exits  
|
Child is re-parented by init/system, which  
automatically calls wait → zombie removed  
Summary: Orphan Process  
gets adopted by init/system  
(PID 1) process  
Parent (PID 4000)  
exits early  
Child (PID = 4001)  
still running
